agent1_raw: This pediatric deterioration model has genuinely strong retrospective performance, but two issues prevent immediate paging activation. First, the outcome labels (ICU transfer or cardiac arrest within 12 hours) are treatment-sensitive — prior proactive rounding and early transfers shape the label distribution, so retrospective AUROC may overstate prospective predictive value once the model changes clinical behavior. Second, page burden — alerts per shift per ward — is not estimated. Alarm fatigue from excessive paging is documented to reduce nurse responsiveness and can cause real harm. The appropriate next step is a prospective silent run to estimate page volume and validate alert predictive value before activating paging.
agent2_raw: The retrospective AUROC is not sufficient for paging deployment, but it is sufficient to justify a prospective silent run. Two key gaps: (1) treatment-sensitive outcome labels — clinician behavior during the training period (proactive rounding, early transfers) shapes whether the label occurs, so retrospective performance may not predict alert utility when the model changes that behavior; (2) alert volume is unmeasured — alarm fatigue is a genuine risk in a pediatric ward already managing multiple monitoring systems, and the threshold page burden per shift needs to be quantified before go-live. Case-mix concentration in chronic patients is a calibration concern that stratified analysis can address. Suppression of end-of-life and ICU-outreach patients is clinically appropriate.
agent3_raw: The model shows promising retrospective performance with cross-site validation, but paging is the intervention, not just prediction. The evaluation asks whether the model predicts deterioration — retrospective AUROC answers this. The deployment question is whether activating paging improves patient outcomes given the alert volume it creates — prospective data with staffing-calibrated thresholds is required. Treatment-sensitive labels (proactive rounding, early clinical transfers contributing to the label) are a secondary concern that the silent run will also illuminate. The safety committee is right to require a silent phase before go-live.
synthesis_raw: All three assessors agree on empirical_test_agreed — a prospective silent run followed by staged deployment is required. The treatment-sensitive label problem and page burden estimation gap are identified by all three. Case-mix concentration is noted by two of three. All three correctly identify suppression rules as clinically appropriate. ETD: condition = prospective silent run then staged deployment measuring page burden, clinician response, ICU transfers, adverse events; supports_critique_if = page volume exceeds staffing limits or alerts don't improve escalation timeliness; supports_defense_if = page burden within limits and alerted patients show improved escalation without excess unnecessary assessments; ambiguous_if = model enriches for deterioration but deployment changes behavior enough that outcome benefit and page burden remain uncertain.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: Treatment-sensitive outcome labels — ICU transfer labels partly shaped by prior clinical concern and proactive rounding, making retrospective AUROC an unreliable guide
Issue 2: Page burden not estimated — alarm fatigue risk from threshold-specific alert volume is the key operational question, not just predictive accuracy
Issue 3: Retrospective positives concentrated in complex chronic patients — calibration and alert rates may differ across broader ward population

empirical_test.condition: Prospective silent trial followed by staged or stepped-wedge ward deployment measuring page burden, clinician response behavior, ICU transfers, and adverse events against prespecified non-inferiority or benefit criteria
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: In a silent run and staged rollout, the chosen threshold would generate page volumes above ward staffing limits, or alerts fail to improve escalation timeliness or adverse-event rates once clinician behavior and suppression rules are accounted for
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: In a silent run and staged rollout, page burden remains within preset staffing limits and alerted patients show improved escalation timeliness or fewer unexpected ICU transfers without excess unnecessary assessments
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: The model enriches for deterioration in silent mode, but deployment changes clinician behavior enough that patient-outcome benefit and acceptable page burden remain uncertain
